answer: The riddle in 'Vainglory' (Old English Elegies, chapter 12, work slug: unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e) contrasts two kinds of men sitting at the mead-hall feast (symbel / wine-hall). The first is the vainglorious man — he swells with pride when wine warms his breast, boasts, deceives, fires arrows of malice at his betters, and is revealed as 'the fiend's own child, wrapped round in flesh.' The second is the humble man — he keeps peace among his kinsmen, loves even his enemy, and is revealed as 'God's own child, welcome in this world.' The poem frames this as a prophet's teaching/riddle: if you meet such a thane dwelling in the halls, you can know by these signs which kind he is — one bound for the abyss, the other bound for the angels' homeland.
